parents.page_3: The story of humanity is defined by continuous discovery and improvement across all possible worlds and timelines. Civilization follows a universal law of failure and rebirth where destroyed worlds are replaced by new ones. While it is impossible to prevent civilizations from falling, one can help new societies grow and prosper by shortening the path from dark ages to renaissance. This book serves as an ultimate guide to rebuilding civilization with the hope that your society remains standing or contributes valuable lessons if it does not.
parents.page_7: Fire is essential for civilization, allowing tribes to gather, prepare food, and access previously unreachable materials. To make fire using the bow method, sharpen one end of a hardwood stick and stand it upright in a hole carved into a plank of wood. Wrap a bowstring around the stick and move the bow back and forth rapidly; this rotation heats the wooden plank until it begins to smolder. The easiest woods to ignite include walnut, cedar, willow, aspen, and pine. Place tinder at the contact point between the rotating stick and the stationary plank to increase friction and produce flames. Effective tinder options beyond dry moss or bark chips include cattails, palm tree husks, flax fibers, and cotton bolls. Gently blow on the fire to supply oxygen, then quickly feed it with wood chips and underbrush once the tinder catches fire. Look for pine trees to find dry branches even during rain, or climb palm trees to access the dry bark husk at the base of their crowns if only palms are available. To create a torch, wrap dry bark, moss, and wood chips tightly around a large branch using a strip of cloth or rope, then saturate it with resin obtained from the outer bark of coniferous trees for several hours of bright light. When building a bonfire, ration resources wisely by piling log ends on top of each other to form an asterisk shape; this arrangement pushes unburnt material to the center as it burns, resulting in a brighter and longer-lasting fire. To save fuel, create a log candle by splitting a pre-dried pine log into four pieces with an ax or saw almost to the base, laying wood chips in the grooves, and igniting them for many hours of heat and light.
parents.page_8: First things after securing shelter is obtaining water, as humans can survive only about seven days without it. Dehydration impairs judgment and reduces the will to live, making an immediate search for water essential. While collecting rainwater or dew is possible, relying solely on these methods is unsustainable without supernatural intervention; therefore, you must identify more reliable sources based on your local climate and landscape. Finding water does not guarantee safety, as many natural sources are not potable and can cause death faster than dehydration if consumed untreated. Almost all natural water sources require filtration and decontamination, particularly stagnant or murky ones, to remove biological threats like bacteria, spores, and parasitic larvae. Larger parasites can be filtered by passing water through sand, wood charcoal, or fine gravel. In tropical regions with access to bananas, the slightly porous peel of the fruit can serve as a makeshift charcoal filter. If you lack a waterproof container for boiling, construct one from birch bark and fill it with heated rocks or river pebbles to purify the water. More complex systems like distillers require advanced societal development. To locate water, look for loose soil where groundwater gathers in valley bottoms, steep inclines, or areas with lush vegetation. In mountainous regions, check fissures at cliff bases near lush plants and dig under dry river beds beneath gravel layers. In deserts, swarms of midges and mosquitoes often indicate nearby groundwater. To delay thirst temporarily, consume juices from trees or plants, ensuring you know which are non-poisonous. Nature provides clues such as large insect colonies like bees and ants, thick juicy plant life, bird flight paths, localized fog, and cracks in rock walls near bird droppings that may lead to hidden water sources accessible with a straw. If natural sources are unavailable, drill a well approximately 10 to 15 meters deep, preferably near alder, birch, spruce, or pine trees, or areas with abundant sedges. To pinpoint the exact location for drilling, dry a large clay pot in the sun and place it upside down; if the bottom begins to sweat, dig at that spot.
parents.page_9: First things while hunger may stimulate creativity, it takes strength to bring your plans to fruition. The answer: plants. Although cooking helps release nutrients, many plants can be eaten raw, so there's no need to waste energy cooking. But to make sure your first bite won't be your last, you'll need to be able to identify edible and poisonous plants. In forest, river or swampy environments with a cool climate, you can eat the bast — a thin layer underneath the bark — of many trees. It is better to look for berries in peat bogs. Blue ones are more likely to be edible than red, yellow or white ones. But don't confuse blueberries with Raven's Eye (Paris quadrifolia), which causes paralysis of the heart muscles. Sweet herbs are safe. Plant tubers are nutritious, as they contain starch. Cereals are a good source of protein and are better when crushed and mixed with water. You can eat pollen and chew the needles of conifers. In arid regions, the best place to look for edible plants is near water. The flesh of sugarcane stalks will satiate your hunger. And cacti will save you from extreme thirst if you wring out the liquid from the pulp. Water can also be found within the fruits and petals of the prickly pear and saxaul bark. Just make an incision in the lower branch of the date palm and liquid will begin to flow out of it. Experiment in the same way with other deciduous trees. To test if a plant is safe, follow the 5–15 rule: Rub a small amount of the plant between your fingers and wait 15 minutes. If this causes no adverse reaction, then rub the plant on the inside of your elbow. If this causes no reaction, then hold a piece of the plant between your lips. If there is no irritation, burning or numbness, then chew on a small part of the plant (do not swallow). Wait 15 minutes. If there is no burning sensation or bitter taste, then swallow this small piece. Wait 15 minutes. If you experience no nausea, dizziness or other symptoms of poisoning, you can eat a small quantity of the plant. If your condition hasn't worsened the next day, the plant is safe to use in food. Umbrella plants, which thrive in damp and overgrown areas, are often inedible. Do not touch the umbrella fronds with your bare hands — this is the most poisonous part. Even plants that are predominantly edible may pack a poisonous punch. Apple seeds, leaves and pits of the cherry, peach, plum, almond and apricot trees contain cyanogenic glycosides, while green potato tubers and leaves contain the alkaloid solanine. But beware of burning poisonous plants in an attempt to destroy them, as inhaling the smoke can be just as poisonous. If you do end up poisoning yourself, flush out your stomach by drinking at least 2 liters of water and inducing vomiting.
parents.page_10: Rope is a critical survival tool that can substitute for many items when necessary, enabling food procurement through fish nets and traps, construction of dwellings without tools, dragging heavy loads without wheels, and staunching bleeding without medication. Suitable materials include wool, animal sinew, hair, silk, and various plants like nettle, hemp, jute, flax, and willow; however, avoid gathering dry plant fibers in open areas as they lack elasticity. To prepare plant fibers, remove leaves from stems and mash them to isolate long, strong strands. When braiding rope, fold a bundle of fibers into a loop that is not exactly in the middle to prevent creating weak spots where material runs out simultaneously on both ends. Press the folded fibers against a flat surface with your palm and slide your hand forward to twist them together, repeating this process to form several bundles into a rope before twisting smaller ropes together for increased thickness. The secret to strong rope lies in twisting the fibers, particularly when braiding. Willow bark is an excellent material for producing extra strong rope; heat the wood with the bark side down over a fire until a faint hissing sound indicates separation, then remove only the stringy, leather-like inner layer. Boil this layer in a mixture of wood ash and water until it turns red, hang it to dry, split the strips into finer fiber strings, and proceed with braiding. Be aware that tying knots can actually loosen rope because fibers on the outer and inner curves experience different tension levels causing uneven deformation; the strongest knots feature alternating left and right bends. To untie tight knots, roll them on a flat surface using your palm or wet the rope to increase fiber flexibility.
parents.page_11: First, master the basics by constructing simple tools from available materials like sticks, stones, ropes, bones, and animal sinew before attempting complex instruments. To create cutting utensils, split large rocks into thin shards; flint is ideal for producing glassy shards with razor-sharp edges that can serve as knives, cutters, scrapers, pokers, or arrowheads. For hunting spears, use hardwood shafts such as oak, ash, or walnut, and smoke them over a fire to evaporate internal water and densify the wood structure. Bamboo is best for fishing spears in shallow waters, but adding two additional prongs to create a trident tip increases your chances of successfully skewering fish. While flint makes sharp and durable knives, obsidian or volcanic glass is better suited for scalpels; however, these materials are fragile and should not be used to hack animal carcasses, reserving them instead for tasks requiring a finer touch. To fell and turn trees, construct an axe using a straight, thick, broad branch wrapped with rope 15–20 cm from one end. Split this section down the middle until reaching the rope, insert a heavy sharp stone between the halves, cross the rope around it many times to secure it, and fix the stone tightly inside to create a working surface. Drilling neat holes is difficult without iron, but you can pour quartz sand into the drill hole; the coarse grains coat your sharp pointed stone instrument with an abrasive layer, allowing you to drill holes in hollow bones to produce needles or hooks. Although gemstones like diamonds lose some value when survival depends on pelts and warm meals, diamonds remain the hardest material in nature and are excellent for creating very durable cutting and sawing tools.
parents.page_12: Start by building simple structures to master basic construction principles before advancing to complex designs. Utilize your environment by using a hillside as a back wall for a hut; dig out the center of a log and line it with stones to create a main support post. Attach a cross beam from this central post to the hillside slope to form a T-frame, then lay branches across it to create a sloping roof that serves as both ceiling and walls. Secure the entire frame with ropes or wire. Cover the roof from above with spruce branches, palm leaves, reeds, or straw mixed with clay. For the fireplace, dig out the hearth body and a wind box on one side for stoking. If placing the hearth inside the dwelling, ensure it is inlaid in the wall or positioned outside to allow air temperature differences to create a draft that forces exhaust fumes through a chimney; insufficient draft can lead to dangerous accumulation of poisonous wood smoke. Insulate the fireplace with raw brick to retain and slowly release heat, or use dried animal dung if firewood is scarce as it burns well and produces significant heat. Orient your house south to north to save energy on heating and cooling, noting that the southern side heats quickly in the Northern Hemisphere while the northern side does so in the Southern Hemisphere. Consider seasonal sun movement and install an overhang to block sunlight during hot summers without obstructing winter light. Place the front door on the side best sheltered from the wind. When constructing an igloo, lay snow blocks in a spiral by shaving the bottom layer at an angle to create the dome shape. Leave a gap at the top for ventilation and ensure the main entrance is a tunnel below floor level to allow gas exchange while preventing heat escape; body heat will melt inner walls slightly, which refreezing strengthens the structure. Light can enter through snowy walls or windows covered with stretched dried animal intestines, and a night light can be made by inserting a wick into a bowl of fish or seal oil. For tipis, use poles from branches thoroughly dried in sun and wind to prevent sagging and leaks. Use treated animal hides soaked in an alkaline solution and stretched for thickness; join pieces with sinew or strong twigs if sinew is unavailable. Create pockets at the top of the hide canvas to hold poles, bind them with leather straps or steamed spruce roots, and secure the structure with a heavy anchor belt tied to a peg driven into the ground. Drive additional pegs through holes along the bottom of the hide for stability and leave a small gap for air circulation. The upper part of the tipi should include a smoke hole with two flaps acting as vents that can be opened or closed using straps or poles, covered by an additional piece of leather when it rains.
parents.page_13: Cleanliness serves as the foundation of both individual health and societal well-being, fostering spiritual purity alongside physical hygiene. Developing a robust hygiene routine is essential, starting with clean hands achieved through soap. While rebuilding civilization requires hard work that may dirty your hands, these surfaces harbor viruses, fungi, and bacteria; therefore, making soap is a critical step in protection. The primary rule of soapmaking is to avoid discussing the process while working, and the second rule involves mixing grease and ash to remove dirt and grease. The chemical foundation of all soap is a mixture of fat and lye, which is an aqueous solution of ash containing mainly potassium carbonate. Processing ashes from plants found in salty semi-deserts or sea coasts yields soda solutions like sodium carbonate. Passing this solution through hard lime, such as limestone burned in a kiln, produces caustic sodium hydroxide. To create lye, construct a filter using wood shavings at the base, followed by a layer of straw, and topped with ash. Pour boiling water over this assembly to allow the lye to drip out from a tap on the side of the barrel. Never store lye in aluminum or tin containers as it will corrode them, and ensure the lye-water mixture does not contact skin or mucous membranes to prevent chemical burns. When pouring liquid soap into molds, use a wooden box lined with wax paper to prevent soaking. Depending on weather conditions and mold size, the soap requires anywhere from a few days to a week to harden and dry before being cut with a string or wire into smaller pieces. The base ingredient for soap is fat, where any vegetable oil works; options like olive oil or coconut oil provide pleasant aromas. Heat oils in a water bath before mixing them with ready lye, working slowly and carefully as the quality depends on this step. Boil the mixture until it reaches the consistency of sour cream, then add salt to cause the soap to bob to the surface. Skim the floating soap ball off the top and repeat this process several times to purify the soap of all lye and oil residue. To create scented and colored soaps, melt freshly made bars in a water bath, preferably after grating them and adding a little water. Once liquid, add essential oils, aromatic herbs, infusions, or natural dyes such as beet juice for pink, cherry or pomegranate for red, or strong coffee for a chocolate appearance. Pour the finished liquid into various molds to harden. Soap is both a practical weapon against germs and a fun activity; dipping a tube in soapy water allows you to blow bubbles, while using a rope loop enables creating bubbles of gigantic proportions.
parents.page_15: Plants have evolved substances to protect themselves from diseases and parasites, and many civilizations have utilized these properties for healing. However, the difference between a remedy and a poison often lies in the dosage. Blue wortweed tinctures made from red flower tips can help with dizziness, limb cramps, and earlobe numbness, while dried and crushed red flower stems serve as an effective laxative. Fresh broadleaf plantain leaves should be crushed to release healing oils for application on wounds, sores, or insect stings; their astringent properties clean the area and reduce inflammation. Young burdock leaves boiled in milk are used to treat rattlesnake bites, while the deep roots possess significant healing power. Burdock root tinctures can reduce high fevers, cleanse the blood, act as a diuretic, and when used as an oil extract, treat rashes and burns. Duckweed, found floating on still-moving ponds, lakes, and wetlands, is best harvested in June or July when it produces flowers. It can be used fresh or dried and ground into a powder to relieve swelling, inflammation, painful urination, reduce flu duration and severity, and promote wound healing. Arctic root grows on the banks of cold mountain streams and rocky slopes in areas with low temperatures and high humidity; it stimulates resistance to physical, environmental, and emotional stressors, may protect cells from damage, regulate heartbeat, and serves as a natural energy drink. For insomnia, prepare a hot infusion using two teaspoons of crushed Valerian root per cup of water and take one large spoonful three to four times a day or half a glass before bed. It is crucial to remember that natural does not necessarily mean safe, so herbs should not be mixed without caution to avoid creating a poison. Nettles have hollow stinging hairs called trichomes that inject histamine upon contact, but their juice helps with purulent wounds, strong tinctures treat rheumatism, and nettle leaf tea relieves headaches, lowers blood pressure, and protects the liver from toxins and heavy metals.
parents.page_16: Humans utilize saunas to cleanse and heal the body by combining earth, air, wind, and fire. The heat and steam increase circulation, flood the system with serotonin to regulate sleep and improve mood, and release endorphins to dull pain, reduce stress, and induce pleasure. Rapid temperature changes achieved by dousing the body with cold water or rubbing it with snow fortify the immune, cardiovascular, and nervous systems while opening pores, promoting lymph drainage, and improving metabolism. Saunas can alter consciousness into a trance-like state and serve as suitable locations for childbirth. The atmosphere of sincerity and vulnerability fostered by nudity and relaxation encourages open communication within the community. A skilled steam room attendant is central to transforming the space into one of healing; they must understand how saunas affect the mind and body and select appropriate plants. Herbs should be harvested at peak ripeness when nutrient and antimicrobial compound levels are highest, then dried and hung in the corner farthest from the stove so that steam releases their aroma and phytoncides. Lightly swatting the body with small branches made of birch, oak, or pine improves blood circulation, massages muscles, opens pores, and promotes sweating; these branches must be soaked in warm water for fifteen minutes and then steamed before use to soften them and revitalize the body with their essential oils. Simple bathhouses can be constructed using a tent or an earth lodge equipped with a stove or a portable bonfire lined with large stones. Smoke should be vented outside to prevent soot accumulation, and users can experiment with humidity, temperature, and steam production methods. Pouring water over heated stones creates fine, dispersed steam that is easy to breathe even at high temperatures without burning mucous membranes or skin.
parents.page_17: The human body is vulnerable to damage from minor incidents like falls or cuts, which can lead to fractures, severe burns, and life-threatening infections such as gangrene and blood poisoning. To treat a fracture, first determine if the bone is broken by checking for puffiness, extreme pain, coldness, or numbness. The most critical step is resetting the bone to its proper position before immobilizing it. If an X-ray is unavailable, use indirect indicators to assess the injury. Create a splint by securing a hard, flat object like a plank to the injured limb, ensuring it extends from one joint above the injury to one joint below it to prevent movement. A gypsum cast offers superior protection; crush gypsum found at dry riverbed mouths into powder and roast it in an open cauldron for 1.5 to 3 hours. Once cooled, mix the powder with water, soak fabric strips in the solution, squeeze out excess liquid, and wrap them around the fracture site starting above and ending below the break. The cast will harden in about 30 minutes. Only apply a cast to bones that have been reset; do not attempt to reset complex fractures where the bone pierces the skin or breaks into multiple fragments, as this risks further damage and infection. In such severe cases, amputation may be necessary for the patient's health. Fractures with no displacement or small fragments typically heal within two weeks for children and three to ten weeks for adults. Burn treatment depends on the severity of the injury. Minor burns causing redness and pain without blisters can be treated by applying ointment and covering the area with an antiseptic bandage, usually healing within a week. Beeswax mixed with honey and aloe vera juice can be melted and cooled to create an effective antiseptic burn ointment. Severe burns that make skin bright red and shiny often develop blisters later; avoid rupturing these blisters to prevent infection spread, then apply ointment and bandage. Burns that turn black indicate severe tissue damage where nerve endings are destroyed; if the affected area is large, a skin graft from a healthy part of the body is required to prevent non-healing wounds, bacterial multiplication, and infection. Clean cuts with even edges heal quickly with minimal scarring, while deep or jagged wounds take longer to heal and produce more scar tissue. All wounds must be cleaned to prevent blood poisoning using soap and water or an alcohol-based antiseptic solution. Remove any foreign matter like gravel, splinters, glass, or shrapnel using tweezers before cleaning from the center outward. Treat the wound with crushed medicinal herbs or honey, which contains hydrogen peroxide and is slightly acidic to fight bacteria. Change dressings regularly to monitor for signs of infection such as bad odor or pulsating pain. If stitches are unavailable, a clean spiderweb can serve as a natural superglue to staunch bleeding, reveal sores and abscesses, and prevent bacterial and fungal growth, provided the web is free of dead insects, bird droppings, and poisonous spiders.
parents.page_18: To extract penicillin, cut unpeeled potatoes into thin slices and submerge them in boiling water for half an hour. Strain the mixture through a clean cloth and add another liter of water along with two tablespoons of sugar and agar made from thawed frozen red algae. Pour this thick liquid into flat-bottomed containers, cover them quickly, and let it thicken. Heat a wire loop over an open fire until hot, then allow it to cool completely so it does not burn the mold. Use the cooled loop to pick up mold and flick it across the surface of the nutrient substrate. Cover the containers and wait one week until drops of yellow liquid form on the substrate; this is your penicillin precursor. To grow the initial mold, place a citrus rind, melon, or piece of bread in a container with a small amount of water, cover it loosely, and leave it in a warm, dark space. A gray mold will appear within a week, turning bright bluish-green when ready. Ensure all containers, tools, and materials are clean and preferably sterilized in boiling water before use. To prepare the medicine, make a loop, heat it, and let it cool. Sterilize an empty glass container. Using the loop, gather the yellow liquid into the container. Heat milk in a flat container until it becomes powdered milk. Add this powdered milk, sea salt, and 100 ml of clean water to the penicillin mixture, close the lid, and shake until dissolved. Before ingestion, verify the substance is penicillin by placing pus or sputum from a sick patient into four jars: add your medicine mixture to two jars and water to the other two. If the mucus in the water grows larger while the mucus with the medicine does not, you have successfully created penicillin with correct proportions. Strain the liquid through a sterile cloth a week later and add the right amount of acid to obtain 100 mg of solution for injection. Treatment should last ten days. Use the medicine immediately after making it as it cannot be stored. Avoid using antibiotics too often, as this causes bacteria to develop immunity, making future infections harder to treat. Do not use penicillin for viral infections; instead, use medicinal herbs.
parents.page_19: While alchemists work to produce the Elixir of Life, surgical intervention may be necessary to save lives by sewing up serious wounds, reattaching missing limbs, or removing problematic organs like the appendix. The primary tool is a sharp scalpel; if unavailable, a thin, well-sharpened blade can suffice. Retractors are used to pry open incisions and access underlying organs. Strabismus scissors cut through vessels, ligaments, and tendons, while needle and thread paired with suture forceps sew wounds back together and grip blood vessels. Always use magnifying glasses when sewing vessels to ensure precision. A mask protects the surgeon from spurting liquids and shields the patient from microbes. A syringe administers pain relievers, and tweezers apply or remove staples during suturing. The appendix is a finger-sized sac attached to the cecum that aids the immune system and digestion but can cause severe pain when inflamed. If inflamed, it must be removed within a few days before it ruptures, causes widespread abdominal inflammation, infects the blood, or leads to death. To perform an appendectomy, work under good light with antiseptics, antibiotics, and pain medication ready. Make an incision deep enough to transect abdominal muscles and the peritoneum. Grasp the cecum and carefully pull it upwards and out through the incision along with the inflamed appendix, doing so slowly to prevent bursting. Pull the appendix aside, tie off the large artery underneath it, and cut through the ligament holding it to the cecum. Tie off the appendix at the base and remove it, then cauterize the wound with a red-hot knife blade. Stitch the wound in layers starting with the peritoneum, followed by abdominal muscles, and finally the skin. Begin stitching before the edge of the wound to anchor it to healthy skin. Use dissolving stitches like catgut sutures for the peritoneum and muscle layers; these can be made from sheep's intestine tissue. Remove the outer layer of stitches one week after the operation. Keep the wound clean and free of inflammation by changing dressings regularly.
parents.page_20: The human circulatory system functions as an interconnected network where blood transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and vitamins to all body parts while removing waste. Blood contains protective elements that fight infections and seal vessel leaks. Red blood cells, or erythrocytes, carry antigens on their surfaces, primarily types A, B, and the Rh factor, which determine blood compatibility. Incompatible transfusions cause these cells to clump into a jelly-like substance, making it vital to match blood types before transfusion. Specifically, individuals lacking the Rh marker cannot receive blood containing it. To perform a transfusion, suspend a glass bottle with a rubber cap upside down above the patient and connect two thin rubber hoses with needles through the cap. Blood drips into a collapsible glass cylinder filter containing a mesh to remove clots before flowing through a tube to the patient's vein. Insert the needle only after blood reaches the tube's end to prevent air bubbles that could cause embolisms. Use an adjustable clamp to regulate flow, while a second tube acts as a pressure vent equipped with an air filter to keep dust out. To determine blood type without equipment, crush lima beans and mix them with salt water to create a seed extract. Mix a drop of blood with saline, spin it, and add a drop of the seed extract; few clots indicate Type A, many clots indicate Type AB, and no clots suggest Type B or O. In emergency situations where time is limited, mix two unknown blood samples in transparent containers; if no clots form, the transfusion is safe, but visible lumps indicate incompatibility.
parents.page_21: Smiling creates a sense of security and indicates a good quality of life, while strong teeth are essential for social thriving and long-term health, preventing issues with the heart, nervous system, and spine. Tooth structure consists of enamel on top for durability and germ protection, dentin beneath it, pulp containing veins and nerves that cause pain during toothaches, roots holding the tooth in the jaw, and gums covering the base to prevent food particles from entering the body. Dental drills operate similarly to sewing machines, using a pedal to transfer energy through a cable to a turbine that rotates the drill bit; higher rotation speeds make treatment less painful by gently removing damaged areas without destroying lower layers, which is why electric drills are preferred. For broken teeth, a threaded post can be inserted into the jawbone to serve as an anchor for a prosthetic tooth, or a porcelain cap can be attached with springs and braces instead of extraction. When multiple teeth need removal, a dental bridge made of durable metal can be affixed to crowns placed on adjacent anchor teeth after they are ground down, while full-jaw prostheses may be crafted from ivory. Any holes or damage must be filled to prevent crumbling, sealing off vessels and nerves to relieve pain; fillings can be made from silver, copper, and tin alloys or dental cement mixed from zinc oxide powder and a binder. The tooth must be clean and dry before applying the filling to ensure adhesion, the material should reach all crevices, and the height must match neighboring teeth to avoid interference with daily life while maintaining the natural shape of the tooth. Drilling into a bare nerve causes severe pain, so anesthesia is necessary; laughing gas can be produced by heating ammonium nitrate in a beaker closed with a cork and small glass tube, releasing nitrous oxide that is bubbled through water and collected in an airtight inverted jar. This gas should be administered to the patient to eliminate pain or induce sleep, but heating must not exceed 250°C to prevent explosion, and because laughing gas is heavier than air, it can be effectively collected in jars or containers.
parents.page_22: Medicine and civilization face a threat if the new generation cannot replace the old, making the reduction of infant mortality during labor a critical priority. Childbirth is a natural evolutionary process where the female body contains all necessary resources to protect and nurture the fetus. When birth begins, the amniotic sac ruptures, releasing fluid through the vagina. A woman is ready to deliver when the cervix opens to 10 cm. The baby's head must be the first part to pass through the vaginal canal; immediately clear its nose and mouth to allow breathing. The shoulders and body follow, after which the umbilical cord should be cut once the pulse stops. The mother then pushes out the placenta, which detaches from the womb wall and must be expelled. If complications arise, such as a breech position, umbilical cord entanglement, or a too-small birth canal, a cesarean section may be necessary. In this procedure, make a horizontal incision above the pubis or a vertical one in extreme emergencies. Cut through the muscle layer, push the bladder aside, incise the uterus, rupture the sac, remove the baby and cut the cord, extract the placenta, and remove the uterus. Stitch the lower and outer layers of the uterus, return it to place, and then stitch the muscle and skin layers. If a C-section is not possible due to complications where the child cannot progress, emergency assistance using a vacuum plunger or forceps is required. For the vacuum method, use a small pump to create negative pressure in a glass bottle with a rubber-lipped suction cup attached to the baby's head, then gently tug to assist delivery. Forceps act as large tongs clasping the child's head; doctors must work with the mother and pull lightly during her pushes, as excessive force can be fatal. A phonendoscope helps diagnose problems by listening to internal organs; it features a thin diaphragm and amplification cone that transmits broader frequencies than a stethoscope and is sensitive to fetal heart tones. Premature babies require an incubator to simulate womb conditions with constant temperature, moderate humidity, and proper oxygen levels. Construct the box from metal and glass for easy sterilization, using heating elements or warm water bottles for warmth from all sides. Install a fan to circulate air with a cotton filter in the duct to prevent microbial contamination. Monitor the baby's weight and temperature constantly without disturbing the sterile environment by placing a scale on the floor and installing rubber gloves sealed into side hatches for access.
parents.page_23: Dense urbanization, trade, and emigration create ideal conditions for epidemics by facilitating cross-contamination between populations with different levels of immunity. To keep civilization afloat during such crises, radical protective measures are essential. Goggles prevent microbes from entering through the eyes when observing patients with pustules or ulcers. Masks stop pathogens from entering via skin and mucous membranes; everyone must wear a two-ply mask with a filter during an outbreak because asymptomatic carriers exist. A cane helps maintain a safe distance of 1.5 meters from contaminated surfaces and can be used to open doors without direct contact. Wear a long hooded robe that covers the entire body, ensuring it is disinfected immediately upon returning home. Gloves protect hands and prevent germs from reaching the face or mouth. High heels keep shoes clean by avoiding contact with contaminated runoff water, reducing the chance of transferring dirt and germs to the skin. An infection rate of one to two cases per week is manageable, but fifteen to twenty cases constitutes an outbreak. When a microbe spreads rapidly from person to person without interruption, it can spread like wildfire across a civilization. If the disease affects multiple settlements, it becomes an epidemic; if it spreads globally to remote areas, it is classified as a pandemic. Breaking the transmission cycle by ensuring infected individuals do not infect others will stop new cases within one or two years. Diseases often originate in forests and are carried by wild animals like bats, rodents, and primates, which can transmit pathogens without falling ill themselves. Avoid building settlements near swamps or rivers where mosquitoes breed and carry diseases. Hunters should work in shifts and live in a separate camp to reduce the risk of bringing infectious agents back to the main population. All water must be purified before use, preferably by boiling to kill microbes quickly. Quarantine is essential; infected individuals must be separated from society, and anyone with close contact to a known case must quarantine for at least seven days or up to twenty-one days even if asymptomatic. Vaccination introduces the immune system to a pathogen in advance using weakened forms of the microbe, providing immunity or drastically reducing symptoms. If technology for creating vaccines is lacking, a risky alternative involves scraping dried crusts or scabs from infected house animals that show similar symptoms. These scabs are crushed into powder and applied via an incision on the patient's hand; if small sores develop after a few days, vaccination was successful. Another dangerous but effective method is variolation, where pus from pimples or pustules on an infected person is transferred via a needle to puncture the skin of a healthy person, typically on the shoulder. While this intentional infection carries risks, it may be preferable to dying from a severe illness.
parents.page_24: To build an X-ray machine, start by constructing an X-ray tube using quartz glass, which can withstand extreme temperatures. Inside the tube, place two electrodes: a cathode made of a metal with a high melting point, such as a tungsten filament from a regular light bulb, and a larger anode made of a metal with high thermal conductivity like copper. Cut the anode stem at a 45 to 70-degree angle and attach a heavy metal platform, such as tungsten or platinum, to it. Remove as much air as possible from inside the lamp to create a vacuum, allowing electrons to travel from the cathode to the anode. When heated, the cathode emits electrons that strike the angled anode platform; this impact causes the electrons to slow down and release energy in the form of X-rays. To prevent the cathode from overheating, provide a constant supply of cold flowing water and submerge the entire tube in an oil bath. Enclose the entire mechanism in a thick lead casing to stop X-rays from escaping in all directions, leaving only one small unprotected window angled toward the tilted anode so the rays can exit. Connect the machine to a high voltage source, preferably one that provides constant voltage. For medical imaging of the stomach or intestines, prepare a contrast agent using barium salts derived from the mineral barite, also known as heavy spar. Since barium does not dissolve in water, grind it into powder and mix it with an algae thickener; add flavoring to make the solution more palatable for the patient. When X-rays pass through the body, soft tissues like muscles and fat absorb very little radiation, while dense materials such as bones or metal objects absorb much more, creating lighter and darker shadows on a photographic plate that reveal bone cracks, swallowed metal objects, or bullets. To protect against ionizing radiation, which can cause burns or cancer in high doses, cover parts of the body not being examined with lead plates. Instead of creating a heavy full-body suit, sew a vest or apron filled with many pockets containing lead plates for practical protection.
parents.page_26: Xotic fruits often require careful identification of edible portions due to unique adaptations. Cashews are technically seeds found inside a soft apple-like fruit; they can be eaten raw for their sweet and sour taste or boiled into compote. The outer shell contains irritating compounds that must not be touched with bare hands, so toss cashews in boiling water for half an hour to neutralize the resin and harden the shell before removing it. Fry the pod afterward to remove the inner shell completely. Cashews contain anacardic acids that kill bacteria, making them useful for preventing tooth decay when chewed. The banana plant is a giant herb, not a tree, and its fruiting body is technically a berry. Bananas can be eaten raw, dried, boiled, or fried. A single tree produces 200 to 300 bananas arranged in tiers on an intricate blossom head that takes ten months to mature. After harvesting, chop down the stalk and plant a new one immediately. Helix beans are actually fruits where ripening causes the skin to burst open, releasing juicy berries that slide down a spiral structure. These berries accelerate as they fall and bounce like rubber balls upon hitting the ground, scattering seeds for multiplication. Harvest the crop before this bursting occurs to prevent scattered berries from taking root or being eaten by wildlife. Peanuts are beans that grow underground in light, sandy soil. Trim bushes frequently because peanut flowers bloom for only one day. Harvest the fruit 90 to 150 days after planting depending on the variant. Peanuts can be eaten raw or fried, used to make cooking oil, meal, hand lotion, laundry soap, shampoo, glue, dye, and ink. Medically, they serve as a laxative or rubbing oil. Coco de Mer fruits resemble hips and appear on palm trees only after twenty years, though the tree can live up to 800 years. This fruit has less liquid and tenderer flesh than coconuts and can be eaten raw or used in dishes, beer, and soft drinks. Coconuts have three distinct shells with agricultural uses. Dry the outer hull for fuel or fertilizer, use the fibrous husk for upholstery, and flake the internal shell for desserts or milk production by mixing it with coconut water. Wait five months for a coconut to ripen before drilling a hole in one of the three small grooves on the outermost shell to drain the liquid. Coconut water is an excellent source of electrolytes and adds flavor to cooking. Use palm tree roots for dye and leaves for weaving mats. The syrup from flowerheads can be evaporated into sugar or fermented into low-alcohol wine. Mangosteen fruits contain segmented pulp with seeds inside. Open ripe mangosteens carefully to avoid staining clothes and skin with inky secretions left by the shell. The pulp provides micronutrients and can be eaten raw, juiced, added to pies, or made into jams and sauces. Fry the seeds and make curd from the peel.
parents.page_27: Mushrooms are fungi that survive by breaking down organic compounds rather than using sunlight for photosynthesis like plants. They reproduce through spores and possess an extensive underground network that supports forest health. Yeast and mold are microscopic fungi used to initiate fermentation for bread, alcohol, cheese, wine, and medicines such as penicillin. Edible mushrooms provide fiber, antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals but are not highly satiating; they can be prepared by boiling, frying, curing, or drying. Even poisonous varieties like fly agaric contain useful compounds including antibiotics, analgesics, and antihistamines, though some species induce hallucinations that alter perception. Mycelium, the root-like structure of mushrooms, can be grown in molds using waste materials like oat hulls to create dense, biodegradable blocks for clothing and packaging that resist moisture and fire; these materials must be dried after growth to prevent further expansion.
parents.page_28: Once you begin growing staple crops, your lifestyle will change drastically as you become dependent on agriculture, allowing you to settle in one place and watch your communities evolve into villages and cities. Both cereals such as wheat, rice, and corn, and pulses like chickpeas and lentils serve as essential staple crops that shape the food, life, culture, and worldview of your civilization. Wheat can be processed into semolina, bulgur, couscous, wheat germ, or flour; its starch serves as a base for ointments and adhesives, while the grain is also required to produce beer, vodka, and whiskey. Corn acts as a staple grain providing food for both people and livestock; unrefined kernels are used in side dishes, soups, or eaten off the cob, while flour can be made into pancakes, cornbread, tortillas, or refined further into cornstarch. Superheating dried corn kernels with oil yields popcorn, and the dried husks and stalks are useful for constructing huts and fences. Chickpeas function as a plant-based meat replacement due to their high protein content and can be prepared into hummus, mashed chickpeas with tahini, or falafel. After harvesting chickpeas, the remaining plant parts serve as livestock feed. Barley grains, once hulled, produce delicious grits or polished pearl barley; they are excellent for thickening soups and stews, essential for brewing beer and whiskey, and make great animal fodder to keep livestock alive through winter. Lentils are drought-resistant legumes high in carbohydrates and protein, forming the basis of salads, earthy soups, stews, vegetarian cutlets, pâté, and sweet dishes like pancakes, muffins, and mockolate truffles; additionally, they fix nitrogen to replenish the soil. Oats tolerate frost and drought well, guaranteeing survival; crushing them yields flour for bread, flattening them creates flakes for muesli, infusing alcohol with oat grains produces a sedative, brewing them offers a coffee replacement, and their broth can thicken baby food and jelly. Cereals can be processed by milling grains into flour, removing the bran shell with a mortar and pestle followed by moistening, steaming, and drying to produce cereals, or soaking and germinating seeds to create malt for beer, whiskey, and other alcoholic beverages. To ensure a steady food source year-round, sow seeds at different times; spring cereals are sown in spring, while winter crops planted in autumn germinate during cold months and ripen earlier than spring crops. Winter crops produce higher yields due to early spring soil moisture reserves but require areas with high snow cover and mild winters, are more demanding on the soil, and are less drought-resistant. Wheat, barley, and rye can all be sown in winter.
parents.page_29: Nature moves in cycles where fields eventually become depleted of nutrients after repeated harvests, so you must practice crop rotation to let the land rest and recover while continuing to produce yields. Interchange soil-depleting cereals with legumes like peas, beans, lentils, annual clover, soy, and peanuts, which saturate the soil with nitrogen. Root crops such as potatoes, beets, turnips, and rutabagas should be grown for both human consumption and animal feed; they act as a caloric storage system that can remain in the ground to be harvested over two years and are not affected by weeding, allowing you to clear unwanted weeds simultaneously. Livestock gain significantly more weight on root crops than on pasture, and their manure rejuvenates the soil. Crop rotation also disrupts the life cycles of pathogens and pests, preventing them from flourishing. Plant wheat and rye immediately after legumes when soil nutrient density is at its peak; use winter varieties sown in winter to germinate earlier and plant them in previously harvested fields to prevent land from standing idle. Grow perennial clover alongside barley and oats, which drain the soil quickly, as the clover grows under the cereal canopy, accumulates nitrogen in its roots, and provides feed for cows and goats. Your best fertilizer source is a compost pit made from food waste, harvest byproducts, and excrement; cover feces with sawdust and straw, leave the mixture for six months to a year while stirring regularly, and dampen it with water to create a slurry that speeds up decomposition. Decomposed sawdust improves soil air permeability and moisture capacity. Plants require phosphorus during germination, nitrogen during active growth, and potassium in preparation for winter. Use sludge from rotting vegetation and marine organisms as a growth stimulator, manure and bird droppings for nitrogen, and powder from boiled and ground animal skeletons or common ash for phosphorus, calcium, magnesium, sulfur, boron, and manganese. Apply fertilizers in early fall to allow slower decomposition that improves soil fertility, and mix peat with lime to improve soil structure.
parents.page_30: To feed a large number of people, you must grow your own cereals and crops rather than just gathering grains. The most critical step is preparing the soil, which requires a plow to transform overgrown weeds into fertile fields quickly. A plow eliminates weeds by severing their roots, turns over the topsoil upside-down, introduces nutrients, and improves drainage and aeration. The plow operates by cutting vertically and horizontally through the ground; the share cuts the soil while the moldboard determines the degree of rotation for turning the earth. The plow frame includes handles, a drawbar, and an optional support wheel to simplify operation. Iron plowshares are triangular and bolted to the frame. After plowing, level the furrows and ridges, scatter seeds, and wait for harvest while managing weeds, pests, fertilization, and watering. If the soil contains stones that damage the plowshare, attach a chisel in front of the share to protect the blade; this can be made retractable to slide forward and back during seeding. A skimmer chops off the topsoil layer with plants and places it at the bottom of the furrow to facilitate plowing and extend the life of the plowshare. Use a harrow to level the soil after plowing and cover seeds with dirt, or use wooden planks with protruding spikes for this purpose. For large areas, employ a mechanical seeder that uses a gear system connected to a wheelbarrow's wheel to rotate a roller inside a grain box, ensuring even seed distribution. Finally, add a thresher consisting of a rotating drum with soft blades to separate grain from chaff and straw during harvest. To cultivate and harvest land efficiently, combine these various devices into a single super-machine, potentially powered by steam.
parents.page_31: Plants exist in a delicate balance where removing even a single grain can cause unforeseen consequences that ripple through time. Birds stealing fruits from your labor should not be scared away, as this may lead to fields being overrun by rodents and insects causing further damage. When waging war against pests, you must avoid upsetting the natural ecosystem. Maintain balance by learning about the lifestyles, habits, and fears of the creatures damaging your crops and use this information to develop an action plan. If you have a source of running water near your field, carve a rocker arm out of a segmented bamboo stalk pivoted slightly to one side of the balance point. The back end should rest on a hard surface like a rock. As the hollow bamboo stalk fills with water, the tube's center of gravity moves past the pivot point. As the tube topples over to dump out the water, the heavier back end smacks against the rock, creating a loud cracking noise that scares away birds. Everyone knows that nature's most frightening predator is man. Take advantage of this reputation by placing a human figure on a pole in a field. You can decorate it with trash that moves or rustles in the wind or shiny objects that glint in the sun. Moving mechanical elements will make your scarecrow even more intimidating. Move the pole to different locations around the field to keep the birds on their toes, or make more scarecrows. The main predator birds have to contend with is other birds. If you haven't had the chance to train a pet falcon to chase away smaller birds picking at your crop, you can make a serviceable imitation using a kite. Humans are not the only creatures prone to superstition; scatter fake dead birds around the field and the living ones will want to stay far away. To fight moles, set the trap by threading the catch ring through the opening in the end of the metal firing arm then mount the arm on the catch. In this position, the tension spring keeps the arm under tension. When the mole sticks its head through the entry ring, its nose will push against the catch ring, sliding it to one side and releasing the catch. This presses the mole's neck against the bottom of the entry ring, rendering it unable to move. Powdery mildew is a type of mold that affects many plants. Spraying with natural antifungals can help. Crush limestone and burn the powder at 900–1200°C. The substance you're left with is calcium oxide, otherwise known as quicklime. Carefully dissolve a kilogram of quicklime in 50 liters of water. Then take a kilogram of copper sulfate, which can be obtained by treating copper with concentrated sulfuric acid heated to 60°C. Dissolve this in a small amount of hot water then add cold water to 50 liters. Mix this with the quicklime solution.
parents.page_32: The type of wine produced is heavily dependent on the soil composition. Brown soils are suitable for sparkling and table wines, while podzolic or ash gray soils are best for light table wines. Humus-carbonate soil, a shallow layer formed over limestone, is ideal for producing high-quality sparkling wines as well as thin vodkas, brandy, and armagnac. Sandy soil allows vines to accumulate sugar, resulting in light wines with a delicate bouquet that are easy to blend. Sierozemic soil, found in white-grey desert areas, produces strong sweet wines. Barros soil, rich in gypsum and carbonic lime, is used for fortified wines, and pink soil is designated for dessert and liqueur wines. Grape vines require both groundwater and rainwater; the latter provides the necessary acidity for a balanced flavor, which is why drier regions produce heavier wines. Sunlight warmth creates sweet and spirituous wines by packing sugar into the fruit, which converts to alcohol during fermentation; approximately 1800 to 2000 hours of sunshine per year ensures a harvest of fully ripened grapes. To protect vines from frost and wind, locate the vineyard on the side of a valley or hill facing the equator, such as a south-facing slope in the northern hemisphere or a north-facing slope in the southern hemisphere. Vines must be pruned to remain frost resistant; young bushes aged one to three years should be pruned in spring when sap flow is absent, while vines four years and older are ready for fruit production. Pruning should occur when shoots exceed 1.2 to 1.5 meters in length. Wine making begins with hand-picked ripe dry grapes that are crushed and left to ferment. Yeast fungi on the grape skins convert sugar into alcohol and carbon dioxide until the alcohol concentration reaches 15 to 18 percent, at which point the yeast dies and fermentation ends. The wine is then carefully poured into oak barrels without disturbing the sediment to infuse for up to two years. While some winemakers may choose less patient methods using vessels corked with oak bark immediately, wine is an art form that combines winemaker skill with the spirit of the location. Excessive wine consumption can throw the body out of balance, leading to emotional crashes and depression rather than enjoyment. The tartness in wine comes from tannins found in the grapevine, seeds, and skin, which bind to saliva proteins and create a dry feeling in the mouth.
parents.page_33: Tea is a highly valuable herb that serves as more than just an energy drink due to its caffeine content; it offers complex flavors best appreciated through mindful consumption known as a tea ceremony. This practice involves unhurried, precise movements, silence, and full concentration on the present moment to break the cycle of everyday life. While rituals vary, the core principles include respect for the drink, careful preparation, and attention to aroma and taste. For black and oolong teas, discard the first steeping and enjoy the beverage after the second infusion. Green teas are best enjoyed from the first steep as they are lighter and more aromatic. To fully experience the flavor, roll the tea around your tongue before swallowing to ensure it covers all taste buds. Timing is critical for both harvesting and brewing; leaves harvested at the precise moment are superior, while delayed harvest results in mediocre quality. Brewing temperatures and times vary by type: use 100°C water for black tea steeped for 3–5 minutes, 80°C for white tea steeped for 1–3 minutes, and 75°C for green tea steeped for 1–3 minutes. All teas are varieties of Camellia sinensis, with differences in aroma and effect determined solely by preparation methods. Cultivated tea bushes should be pruned to keep only the top two leaves and a bud, as lower parts produce inferior quality. The production process involves four main stages: drying spread across a cloth in a warm room for six hours to wilt the leaves; fermentation which is a biochemical oxidation reaction where black tea undergoes long fermentation, green tea has short fermentation stopped by heating, and white tea is not fermented; roasting in an oven at 90–95°C for about an hour followed by drying; and sun-drying trays. For white tea specifically, leaves are wilted and immediately dried to prevent fermentation. During the fermentation stage, roll leaves until sticky and curl them into tubes, stack them 7–10 cm high in a container covered with a damp towel for about eight hours, and halt the process with heat once the strong smell of tea indicates completion.
parents.page_34: Preserve your happiest and most comforting memories by filling your home with the scent of fresh coffee; brewing a cup will evoke feelings of well-being and peace regardless of your location. When processing coffee beans, wash-processed coffees tend to have a pronounced sourness while dry-processed beans are sweet. For wash processing, use a depulper with an iron box containing a roller with teeth to squeeze the berries against the wall, freeing the beans from the pulp which falls to the bottom; subsequently soak the beans in water to remove excess pulp during fermentation. For natural or dry processing, leave the berries to dry in the sun while periodically turning them to prevent mold formation, noting that this method requires more time and sunlight than usual. Once dry, separate the pulp and roast the beans in a rotating drum over a fire; remember that the roasting process affects acidity, sweetness, and bitterness, with darker roasts resulting in less acidity and more bitterness. To grow coffee shrubs, sprout seeds in a planter before transplanting seedlings into an area with low wind surrounded by larger trees for shade from direct sunlight. The tree will bloom in the third year and produce red berries by the fifth year; prune the shrubs to keep them no taller than 2–3 meters for easier harvesting. The ideal location for growing coffee is warm and humid with dappled sunlight and a stable temperature between 17 and 23 degrees Celsius, using loose, fertile, well-watered soil with good acidity. High-altitude coffees taste complex and acidic while lower altitudes produce more aromatic varieties. If you are too groggy in the morning to make coffee manually, use a coffee maker consisting of a container with two detachable parts and an air-tight seal; fill the lower tank with water, hang a funnel from the top of the lower chamber, pour ground coffee into a mesh compartment between the tanks, and attach an inverted funnel on top. Place the kettle on the fire so that boiling water in the lower tank turns to steam, increasing internal pressure and pushing water up through the funnel; this water passes through the coffee in the mesh filter to absorb flavor and aroma before shooting out the upper funnel to collect as an espresso shot in the upper chamber ready for pouring. Coffee is a stimulant consumed for its energy-giving properties, and to maximize revitalizing effects, take a 20-minute power nap right after drinking your cup so you benefit from sleep before the caffeine kicks in. Caffeine blocks the action of adenosine, a signaling molecule that makes you feel fatigued and drowsy, thereby invigorating the mind and helping you stay awake. Additionally, caffeine causes increased levels of dopamine in the reward centers of the brain, delivering a mild high but also creating an addictive effect.
parents.page_35: The secret to chocolate's power lies in its ability to balance flavors, unifying sweet and salty or bitter and spicy tastes to create harmony and store energy. To cultivate cacao, collect ripe seeds from wild trees and sprout them in bamboo tubes. Plant the shoots in the shade of tall trees to protect them from direct sunlight and wind. Trim ripening buds off the plant until it reaches five years old to ensure a heartier tree. Peel ripe fruit away to extract the seeds, known as cacao beans, then remove the pulp. Spread the beans on banana leaves and leave them covered for a few days, shuffling them regularly. After two weeks, the seeds will darken and gain flavor and aroma; dry them in the sun and roast them over a fire or in an oven until the outer hull bursts and can be peeled off. Grind the beans into a thick cacao paste, which serves as the base for chocolate. To make white chocolate, use cacao butter without cacao solids. When cooking the mixture to make it homogeneous and evaporate excess moisture, stir continuously for about five days at a temperature of 85–90°C to eliminate extraneous odors and allow the pure chocolate aroma to shine through. To create chocolate bars, melt the cacao paste until liquid at 42–45°C, avoiding higher temperatures that would cause loss of elasticity. Cool the mixture to no more than 31°C and incorporate solid cacao butter obtained by squeezing the paste under a press; note that cacao butter is hard and brittle below 25°C but becomes liquid above 32°C. Break the cacao butter into small granules and mix them into the paste. Dip a knife into the mixture; if done correctly, the chocolate mass on the knife should become dull and harden within one and a half minutes. Pure cacao paste is aromatic but very bitter, so add powdered sugar to sweeten it or pour in milk or cream to make milk chocolate. Pour the finished chocolate into molds and refrigerate. Adjust the ratio of bitter to sweet or mix in ingredients such as nuts, fragrant flowers, freeze-dried berries, cinnamon, vanilla pods, cardamom, pepper, or sea salt. Substitute almond, coconut, or rice milk for animal milk, or use honey instead of sugar. Thanks to the cacao butter, the mass crystallizes and hardens but melts upon contact with the tongue, releasing a full bouquet of flavors and aromas. Alternatively, grind roasted beans into a powder and add hot milk, sugar, and spices to create a fortifying pick-me-up drink.
parents.page_36: Plants face difficulties with geography, soil quality, climate, weeds, and pests, but these challenges should not prevent agriculture. Large-scale production of cereals, tea, grapes, vegetables, and herbs is possible without vast flat fields by utilizing hillside terraces or vertical farms. Rice can replace bread and other grains as a dietary staple; its grains yield starch, flour, alcohol, and wine, while the germ produces oil. Rice straw serves as paper pulp or mattress filler, rice flour forms the base for cosmetic powders, blushes, oils, creams, and scrubs, and the bran provides feed for livestock and poultry. Ducklings and fish raised in paddy fields contribute protein to your meal. On hilly terrain, construct mini-fields on slopes shaped like broad steps, reinforced with clay bumpers to prevent fertile topsoil from washing away. Dig irrigation channels with drainage openings and rotate crops annually to avoid soil depletion, such as switching between tea, grapes, and corn. Periodically plant Azolla, an aquatic fern whose roots host nitrogen-absorbing bacteria that act as natural fertilizer. Rice cultivation requires a nearby river or lake for gravity-fed irrigation via canals or hoses. Protect rice shoots from strong direct sunlight and weeds; maintain water levels covering less than half the plant to avoid unnecessary waterlogging, draining periodically through canal systems. Flood fields preemptively before planting to wash away unwanted seeds and inhibit weed growth. Soak rice seeds in water for 12 hours, though waiting until sprouts reach 2 cm is preferable. Keep fields free of weeds like reeds and sedges by wading knee-deep or introducing ducks and fish that eat harmful insects and weeds while fertilizing the soil. Rice ripens in 3–4 months; when it reaches about 40 cm in height, drain the water or allow it to evaporate. After a couple of weeks, yellowing indicates readiness for harvest. Cut rice ears or pluck them whole with roots, then dry for 2–3 days before threshing the husk. Sand grains by rubbing them together in a mortar or rotating barrel to whiten brown rice, noting this improves appearance and digestibility but reduces nutrient content. Vertical greenhouses allow farming regardless of weather or soil nutrients by arranging plants on moving carousels that ensure equal light and water exposure. Use substrates like coconut husk or shredded tree bark instead of soil, immersing roots directly in nutrient solutions to save energy for ripening. Place water containers above plants to use gravity for irrigation, optionally using a pump. In rooms with glass walls, utilize natural light supplemented by mirrors reflecting light from all directions; install wire grids to redirect growth horizontally and prevent shading while stimulating new buds. Ensure air circulation to control humidity but avoid sudden temperature changes by installing a water heating system.
parents.page_38: Time management is vital in extreme conditions where a single second can determine survival. Instead of spending hours or days crafting weapons or hunting, you should set traps and complete other important tasks like chopping wood, fetching water, or building shelter while waiting. The main concept behind any trap is creating an entrance with no exit, requiring no complex tools as nature provides everything needed. Deciding where to place your trap is the most important part. To catch predators attacking your herd, build a wolf pen by driving long stakes into the ground to create two circular fences. The inner ring should be 3–4 meters in diameter and solid with no breaks, while the outer ring should be around 45 cm larger. Install a gate in the outer ring that swings only inward so the predator can move forward freely but cannot turn around. Lure the predator using live bait such as a goat kid, goose, or piglet. For catching foxes or weasels stealing chickens, use a trap consisting of a stick that keeps the upper part suspended, a notch it fits into, and bait like grouse meat. As the animal nudges the stick to snatch the meat, the mechanism releases and falls to crush the prey; this principle can be adapted using ropes instead of sticks as long as the trigger is nudged by the animal taking the bait. To catch flies spoiling food, make a box where the bottom wall forms a pyramid sticking into the box with a hole at the top. Insects fly in through the hole but cannot find their way back out. Place meat inside as bait, preferably not too fresh. To catch grouse, dig an angled pit with an opening wide enough for a grouse to crawl through. Place bait such as rowan berries, lingonberries, or cranberries on the bottom. The grouse will crawl in but lack the space to turn around and spread its wings, making it incapable of walking backwards. To set a trap for foxes raiding gardens and killing rabbits or hares, drive a peg into the ground with a notch in the middle. Tie a self-tightening loop to a flexible tree branch or long stick, bend the branch downward, cut a small notch in the end, and latch it into the peg's notch. Place the loop in an area with high fox foot traffic; as the fox runs through, it triggers the spring-loaded branch which tightens the noose around its neck. To catch mice, dig a hole 30–45 cm deep with a wide bottom and narrow entrance. Construct a roof over the entrance from bark or wood supported by small pebbles. Mice will try to hide under this shelter and fall into the hole, unable to scale the extreme angle of the pit walls.
parents.page_39: Master the art of fishing as a quiet and safe method of hunting that rarely becomes dangerous, provided you avoid becoming hooked by dopamine from the thrill of the catch. To craft a fishing rod, select a long stick made of bamboo or flexible wood such as birch, willow, bird cherry, or maple. Attach a fishing line composed of interlaced silk threads to the rod and fasten a barbed hook, which can be made from bone or metal, to the end of this line. Bait your hook using real food or artificial lures designed to imitate the flash of scales, water beetles, leeches, or crustaceans. For stagnant waters, use bran, bread, or steamed cereals as bait, while for rivers, mix live bait like bloodworms or maggots with clay enhanced by scents such as vanilla, cinnamon, or dill to attract fish through scent; ensure you practice moderation so the bait attracts attention without overfeeding. You can also attach a bait ball directly to the hook by bending metal wire into a spiral and molding the ball around it. To help the hook sink below the water's surface, tie a sinker made of pebble or iron to the line, and place a bobber made of bark or cork a short distance up the line to signal when a fish takes the bait. If you wish to fish far from shore, add a reel to your rod, wind silk fishing line around it, tie a hook to the end, and attach your bait; the spinning drum releases the wound line to propel the hook over long distances, while a brake lever prevents tangling when the bait splashes down. A lighter reel with lower friction on its axis allows for longer casts, and you can rotate the handle to retrieve or slowly reel in the baited hook to attract fish. For catching large quantities of fish to feed many people, construct bigger traps using woven baskets filled with bait immersed in water, or weave nets by cutting wound thread or rope into long pieces, laying half parallel and the other half perpendicularly while securing each intersection with a knot; build wooden pole levers to help raise these nets. Squid are best caught at night as they respond well to luminous objects, so highlight your bait by pointing a flashlight at it.
parents.page_40: At a certain stage of development, you will face difficult ethical questions regarding eating living beings, but the need to survive makes these decisions easier. Enjoy the advantages of being at the top of the food chain, but treat the animals you kill with respect and do not waste any part of their body. Hollow horns can be used to make musical instruments and wine goblets. The brain should not be used for food due to the high risk of contracting disease, but it can be used for tanning leather. The guts and veins are good for making bowstrings and strings for musical instruments; to do this, thoroughly wash the intestines and veins, scrape off the external membrane and any excess fat, soak them in cold water, cut them into long strips, then stretch and twist them until you get strings of the desired length and thickness. Hooves make for a rich stew, and the hard outer shell or sheath can be carved into small trinkets such as buttons, combs, and ornaments. To remove the sheath, immerse the hoof in boiling water for an hour, drain it, place a weight on top to let it cool, then remove the remnants of the skin and soft tissues. Treat the sheath with a knife, fine-toothed or coarse files. Blood is a valuable raw material rich in protein, vital amino acids, phospholipids, iron, and other substances that play an important role in how our body functions; you can use it to make smoked blood sausage. Boil the bones to make glue or grind them into bone meal, which is an excellent fertilizer. If you burn the meal, the resulting ash can be added to white clay to obtain bone china, an extra thin and durable type of ceramic. Fur is an excellent heat insulator and produces fur coats that can withstand severe frost up to negative 60 degrees Celsius. Leather is not only useful for shoes, pants, bags, gloves, raincoats, and furniture; it is a strong, very durable material and can be used to make drive belts for various mechanisms. The skin of young lambs, goats, and cows can be used to make parchment, which can be used in place of paper or stretched into a skin for percussion instruments. After you have removed the skin from the carcass of the animal, you need to thoroughly clean it of any meat and blood remnants. Soak the hide in saline first to make this process easier. After that, the skin needs to dry as quickly as possible, so stretch it between two poles and leave it to dry in the sun. Remove the hair using a process known as liming, during which the hides are treated with lime and sodium sulfate. Lime is obtained by roasting calcium-magnesium rocks such as chalk or limestone. You can also use a saline solution at a ratio of 20 grams of salt to 1 liter of water; soak the skin in the solution for several days, turning it over from time to time. After this procedure, any hairs and residue of subcutaneous tissue can be easily removed. After curing, many substances will remain in the skin that must be disposed of. To do this, rinse the skin in a soda solution with 10 grams per liter of water, then rinse your skin with clean water several times. Leather becomes more wear-resistant, heat-resistant, and resilient if tanned. Extract tannins from oak, willow, spruce, and larch woods or from the growths formed on oak leaves. Another option is the fats of various marine animals. Fill a rotating drum with a strong tannin solution and soak the hides, then squeeze out to remove excess water and repeat the process. Now the leather can be rolled out, dyed, sanded, varnished, and sewn into the items you need. If you do not have access to any of the sources of tannin, stretch the hide across pegs and rub it with a mixture of urine and clay. Leave it for a while, then treat it with fat or oil, scrape it, and smoke it over a fire.
parents.page_41: Each barn must contain drains for urine that lead to an underground container surrounded by concrete. You can make organic fertilizer from manure and water the fields with urine. The feed storage room must be protected from moisture. Most livestock can eat hay, silage, legumes and grains. Feed birds birch branches and nettle leaves in the summer, which will save them from vitamin deficiency and allow them to fly faster in winter. A mud pit is a necessity for any hog farm. Pigs don't have sweat glands, so they need to wallow in mud to effectively regulate their body temperature. Chickens need an ash pit to clean their skin and feathers of parasites. But make sure to dilute 1 part ash with 2 parts river sand and 100 grams of sulfur, as concentrated ash contains potash and soda that can irritate the skin and mucous membranes of birds. Make sure your pigsty has good lighting in the fall/winter. Pigs need regular sun exposure to gain weight. To form fat and improve the taste of meat, add lake sludge to the feed — it contains the necessary elements: copper, calcium, zinc, sulfur, magnesium. Many diseases in livestock are caused by insect bites. The most vulnerable place on a cow's skin is the udder. Treat it with a cream of laurel, menthol and eucalyptus essential oils. This will repel insects and soften the skin of the udder, which will keep it from cracking. That means your cow will have a consistently high milk yield. Standing in one place for a long time makes the horse's hoof soft. Because of this, the cornified hoof tissue is quickly ground down, which causes pain. Strengthen the hooves by nailing horseshoes to the bottom to protect the living tissue. The inner layer of the horny wall of the hoof is delineated by a clearly visible white line. This tissue is insensitive enough to drive nails into. To maintain your herd, you need to ensure it contains both males and females of reproductive age. An effective way to raise animals with good meat, wool and milk is to selectively breed them. Select the best members of your herd and breed them using artificial insemination: collect semen and inject it into the female's cervix during ovulation. A large syringe will do the trick. Share biomaterial with neighboring farmers to help avoid inbreeding and make the stock healthier. Vaccinate herds against rabies and other diseases. If one animal falls ill, isolate it immediately. Sheep can be quite skittish. Graze them together with horses or goats to make sure they don't run off at the slightest rustle of a bush. Sheep copy the behavior of other animals and will feel more protected. Sheep are quite hearty and can live outdoors in temperatures down to –15C°. Any lower than that and they need a heated room. Sheep hooves are quite fragile, so the floor must be planked with wood for the flock to stay healthy. To ensure a steady supply of eggs, supplement your chickens' diet with salt and chalk to make the eggshell denser. Sour milk and herbs make good feed for laying hens. The wool of the goats will be thicker and of better quality, and the milk yield higher, if you treat the goat's skin with a solution of soda at least once a week. This prevents parasites from nesting in the wool.
parents.page_42: To begin producing silk, mate your silkworm moths. The females will lay eggs approximately two to three days later, and the larvae will hatch eight to ten days after that. Move the hatched larvae to a well-ventilated area and feed them fresh mulberry leaves regularly. After about thirty days, the larvae will transform into caterpillars. When the caterpillars turn yellow and become slightly transparent, they are ready to pupate. Provide them with a tray equipped with pegs at the bottom. Within three to four days, the caterpillars will start spinning their cocoons around these pegs. A single thread can be up to 1.5 kilometers long. However, you must interrupt this process after about fifteen days; otherwise, a new moth will burst through the cocoon and ruin the continuous thread. To keep the threads intact, place the cocoons in boiling water. This step kills the pupa inside and dissolves the protein sericin that binds the silk threads together. Carefully brush each cocoon to locate the end of the thread. Once you have identified the ends on six to eight cocoons, gather the threads together, pass them through a hole in the middle of a plank of wood, and wind them around a spool. As you rotate the spool, the cocoons will unwind. Rinse the braided yarns in soapy water to remove excess sericin and other substances, then rinse again with hot water. Your silk thread is now ready for use. It can be used to make strings for musical instruments, thread for suturing wounds, or wire winding. You can also weave silk to create parachutes, sails, and even armor. The triangular cross-section of the silkworm thread causes it to refract light like a prism, giving silk its characteristic shine.
parents.page_43: Bees represent a model of waste-free production where every product, including honey and wax, is utilized while simultaneously aiding plant reproduction through pollination. To make candles, twist cotton thread into a wick and repeatedly dip it into melted wax until the desired thickness is achieved, or alternatively, fix a wick to the sides of a clay or glass mold before filling it with wax to allow for mixing colors and scents. Beekeeping yields various products such as wax for cosmetics and polishes, royal jelly from nurse bees for cosmetic use, and bee bread which consists of pollen and nectar balls; this can be harvested by freezing honeycomb, removing the honey layer, grinding the comb, and sifting through a fine mesh to separate the frozen wax from hard pollen granules.
